title: The Stillest Man in the Room
author: Fablewick
lesson: A steady person can change a noisy room.
summary: In a year when every bank in the country was about to fall over, a serious old man named J.P. Morgan sat very still in a library and refused to leave until the panic was over.

1.label: Little Listener
1.text:
In a big, loud city, there lived a quiet boy.

His name was Pierpont. He liked numbers. He liked hush. While the city rattled and rang, Pierpont sat still and counted.

The boy grew up. The beard grew white. And Pierpont became the most famous banker in the land.

Then one autumn, everyone got scared at once.

Run to the bank! Knock on the door! I want my money, all of it, now!

But there was not enough money for everyone. The banks began to wobble. The whole country began to wobble.

So Pierpont opened his library, the room with all the old books, and called the bankers in.

They argued. They shouted. They jumped up to go.

Click. Pierpont locked the door.

He sat in his big leather chair. He did not shout. He just looked.

"Nobody leaves," he said, "until we fix this."

The clock ticked. The candles burned low. Pierpont sat still, still, still.

And at last, the bankers agreed. They put their money together and held the banks up.

The wobbling stopped.

One still man. One noisy room. And a whole country, back on its feet.

2.label: New Reader
2.text:
Long ago, in a big, loud American city, there lived a quiet boy named John Pierpont Morgan. Everyone called him Pierpont.

Pierpont liked numbers better than games. He liked quiet rooms better than crowded ones. While the city outside rattled with horse carts and shouting, he sat at his desk and worked his sums, all the way down the page.

The boy grew up. The beard grew in, and then it grew white. And by the time Pierpont was an old man with small round glasses, he was the most famous banker in the whole country.

Then one autumn, when Pierpont was seventy, something bad happened.

It was called a panic.

A panic is when everyone gets scared at the very same time. People worried that the banks had lost their savings. So they ran to the banks. They stood in long, cold lines around the block. Give us our money, they said. All of it. Right now.

But banks lend money out — that is what banks do — so they could not hand everything back at once. One bank wobbled. Then another. And if the banks fell over, the whole country would fall over with them.

Pierpont heard the news at his country house. He packed a bag and rode the long train home. He didn't say much. He watched the window and thought.

Back in the city, he did not give a speech. He did not write to the newspapers. Instead, he opened his own private library, the hushed room with the rare old books, and sent word to every important banker in the city: Come. Tonight.

They came. They sat among the tall shelves, in chairs that creaked. They argued. They shouted. They blamed each other. Some stood up to leave.

Click. Pierpont locked the door.

He sat back down in his big leather chair, the one shaped almost like a throne, and folded his hands. He did not shout. He did not pound the table. He looked at them, one by one, over his small round glasses.

"Nobody leaves this room," he said, "until we have decided how to fix this."

The clock ticked. The candles burned down. The bankers grumbled and argued and grumbled some more. Pierpont sat still.

He sat still for hours. He sat still for days.

And at last, near dawn, the bankers agreed. They put their own money together, all in one pile, and used it to hold the wobbling banks up until the fear passed.

The country did not fall over.

In the middle of all that noise, one steady man had refused to move, and the noise had finally worn itself out against him.

3.label: On Your Own
3.text:
Long ago, in a big, loud American city called New York, there lived a quiet boy named John Pierpont Morgan. His first and middle names together were too much of a mouthful, so everyone simply called him Pierpont.

While other boys ran shouting through the streets, Pierpont was usually inside with a book or a page of sums. He liked numbers better than games. He liked quiet rooms better than crowded ones. When he read a book, he read it all the way to the end, even when it was long and dull. And when he made up his mind about something, it stayed made up.

Pierpont grew. He went to school in America, then in Switzerland, then in Germany. He grew tall, grew a beard, and went into the family business, which was banking. He was so quietly, stubbornly good at it that by the time the beard had turned white, by the time he wore small round glasses and carried a stare that could make a grown man forget his own name, Pierpont Morgan was the most famous banker in the whole country.

Then one autumn, when Pierpont was seventy years old and thinking seriously about slowing down, something terrible happened.

It was called a panic.

A panic, in the language of money, is what happens when everyone in a country gets frightened at exactly the same time. It went like this: a few banks in New York made some risky bets and lost. One bank wobbled, and suddenly people began to worry about all the banks. Worried people hurried to take their money out. But banks lend money out — that is what banks do — so they could not hand everything back at once. The lines on the sidewalks grew longer. The newspapers shouted. The worry spread like weather.

Then the worry became something worse. Banks began closing their doors: some because the money was gone, some because they feared it would be gone by morning. By the middle of October, it looked as if every bank in America might fall over at once, each one knocking down the next, like a long line of dominoes.

And if the banks fell, the country fell.

Pierpont was at his country house when the news came. He listened without a word. Then he packed a small bag, ate a slow dinner, and took the long train back to New York. He smoked a cigar. He watched the dark fields slide past the window. He thought.

Back in the city, he did not give a speech. He did not write angry letters to the newspapers. He did not stand on any stage.

Instead, he climbed the steps of his own private library, the grand stone room behind the heavy bronze doors where he kept his rare old books, and sent word to every important banker in New York: Come here. Tonight. Without fail.

They came. They had to. By now, everyone knew that Pierpont Morgan was the one man all the others were secretly watching.

The library was a strange place for a meeting. It was hushed and tall, lined with old books that smelled of leather and dust. Stern faces looked down from the paintings on the walls. The chairs creaked whenever anyone shifted. The whole room felt as if it had been built, long ago, to make loud people feel a little embarrassed about being loud.

The bankers sat. Then they argued. They shouted across the table. It's his fault, said one. The government should fix it, said another. And after an hour or two, a few of them simply stood up and headed for the door, because they were tired and could not see a way out.

Pierpont rose, walked to the door, and turned the key. Click.

Then he went back to his big leather chair, the one at the head of the room, shaped almost like a throne, and sat down. He did not pound the table. He did not raise his voice. He looked at the men, one by one, slowly, over his small round glasses.

"Nobody," he said, when the room had finally gone quiet, "leaves this room until we have decided how to fix this."

And then he waited.

He waited through arguments that flared up and burned out. He waited through midnight, and through morning, and through more meals and more cigars than anyone could count. Some men slept on the couches. Some paced the carpet. Outside, the country held its breath. Inside, Pierpont sat still.

At last, because they had nowhere else to go, and because Pierpont would not move, the bankers decided. They put their own money together, every one of them, into one great pool, and they used it to hold the wobbling banks upright until the panic passed.

The country did not fall over.

Afterward, books were written about Pierpont Morgan, and not all of them were kind. Some said no one man should hold that much power. Others called him a hero. Pierpont, who did not much care for arguments about himself, went back to his numbers and his quiet, careful work.

But every man who had sat in that library, under that stare, in those creaking chairs, remembered the same thing.

In the middle of all that noise, one steady man had refused to move.

And the noise, in the end, had worn itself out against him.
